channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Coming to a Blood Donation Center Near You: the Chicago Symphony
item.description: When the medical technology company Abbott developed mixed-reality games, it got one of the world’s great orchestras to record the soundtracks.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Edgar
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: Margaret Wiegel, who gives blood as often as every three weeks, testing out Abbott’s mixed-reality games while donating recently.

item.title: At the Kennedy Center, a Name Change Shrouded in Uncertainty
item.description: President Trump’s name was removed from the arts institution’s facade overnight on Saturday. Many questions remain, including whether or not it stays off.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Rahmat Gul/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Kennedy Center certified on Saturday that President Trump’s name had been removed from the building, but did not give a clear answer on when the tarps would be removed.

item.title: Tyra Banks Sues Netflix for Defamation Over ‘America’s Next Top Model’ Docuseries
item.description: The lawsuit claims that producers used roughly 16 minutes of Ms. Banks’s three-and-a-half-hour interview to present a false narrative stripped of context.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Reality Check: Inside America's Next Top Model (TV Program)

item.title: Emily Blunt on ‘Disclosure Day’ and Feeling Flappable Around Spielberg
item.description: The star grew up on the director’s films and tried “to really compose myself” when he invited her to a meeting about a secret movie.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I remember being completely traumatized by ‘Jaws’ and thrilled by ‘Indiana Jones,’” Emily Blunt said. “So it is a very surreal thing” when Steven Spielberg wants to work with you.

item.title: How David Hockney Taught Los Angeles to See Itself
item.description: A Brit, he became a symbol of the city’s culture, stylish and alienating, with his vivid swimming pool paintings and embrace of SoCal light, hedonism and gay liberation.
item.dc:creator: Jori Finkel
item.media:credit: Nathanael Turner for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Hockney repainted his own pool — and signed and dated it July 8, 2017, the day before his 80th birthday.

item.title: David Hockney and the Bliss of Not Standing Still
item.description: “As important as the boys and the pools and the light,” a memoirist writes, “the most important thing was becoming the driving.” It would inspire an obsession with moving focus into the future.
item.dc:creator: Lawrence Weschler

item.title: Vandals Damage Work by Black Artist at Houston Museum, Officials Say
item.description: Two men scraped and punctured a $23,000 painting at the Houston Museum of African American Culture last month, the museum said. It decided to display the damaged artwork.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Houston Museum of African American Culture
item.media:credit: Houston Museum of African American Culture
item.media:description: The damaged painting, “Man in the Garden” by the artist Clarence Heyward, was part of an exhibition called “Eden.”

item.title: The Violence in Iran Was Real. The Film Showing It Was A.I.
item.description: The docudrama “Dreams of Violets” premiered at the Tribeca Festival, a sign that some in Hollywood have warmed to the technology.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez and Matt Stevens

item.title: Jack White, Sylvan Esso: Songs to Know This Week
item.description: Jack White makes his slide guitar scream, Sylvan Esso rocks out and 7 more new tracks to know.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Leon Neal/Getty Images
item.media:description: Jack White — who just presented his first exhibition of art in London — returns with a new single ahead of a new album in July.

item.title: What to Know About the U.F.C. Fights at the White House
item.description: About 4,300 spectators will surround an octagonal cage on the South Lawn on Sunday night. President Trump and Dana White, the U.F.C.’s chief executive, are longtime friends.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.F.C. fights at the White House are scheduled to begin at 8 p.m. Eastern time on Sunday and will be shown on Paramount+.

item.title: Olivia Cooke of ‘House of the Dragon’ Shares Her Comfort Items
item.description: She hates when the mugs on offer at a hotel are “teeny-weeny.” “So I just bring my own massive one,” she said.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Shane Anthony Sinclair/Getty Images

item.title: Steven Spielberg on How to Build Suspense in ‘Disclosure Day’
item.description: The director narrates a scene from his movie that is a continuation, of sorts, of a sequence he filmed for his feature-length debut, “Duel,” in 1971.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: Slayyyter Had One Last Chance for Pop Stardom, and She Nailed It
item.description: After a few attempts that didn’t fully connect, the St. Louis singer was ready to walk away, but not before making a raw album of thrashed pop, “Worst Girl in America.”
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica, Joe Coscarelli and Caroline Tompkins
item.category: Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival

item.title: Review: Olivia Rodrigo’s ‘You Seem Pretty Sad for a Girl So in Love’
item.description: On “You Seem Pretty Sad for a Girl So in Love,” the 23-year-old singer delivers a polished, solemn breakup chronicle that misses her signature adrenaline rush.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.category: You Seem Pretty Sad For a Girl So in Love (Album)
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: Olivia Rodrigo’s third album documents a relationship, from beginning to end.

item.title: Las Culturistas Culture Awards and 6 More Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: Bowen Yang and Matt Rogers host their pop culture award show, and “Summer House” airs a surprise episode.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Monty Brinton/Bravo

item.title: Singer Oliver Tree Is Said to Have Died in Collision of Helicopters in Brazil
item.description: The two helicopters crashed in Rio de Janeiro on Sunday, killing at least six people, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:credit: Tercio Teixeira/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The helicopters crashed over a car dealership in Rio de Janeiro on Sunday.

item.title: The Knicks Effect: Irresistible, Spontaneous Dancing
item.description: The Knicks turned New York City into a stage, where movement is a catalyst for untainted happiness.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas

item.title: ‘Disclosure Day’ Ends Spielberg’s Summer Box Office Drought
item.description: Steven Spielberg’s original science-fiction movie collected an estimated $44 million in North America, giving the director his first new-to-the-screen summer hit in 24 years.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Roy Rochlin/Getty Images for Universal Pictures
item.media:description: Universal Pictures’ promotional campaign for “Disclosure Day” sought to remind summertime audiences that Steven Spielberg was capable of delivering a major event movie.

item.title: Jane Yolen, Whose Books for Children Drew on Everyday Life, Dies at 87
item.description: She wrote some 450 books, including novels, poetry and nonfiction in many genres. One critic called her “a modern equivalent to Aesop.”
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Beth Gwinn/Getty Images
item.media:description: Jane Yolen in 1993. Her best known books include “Owl Moon,” which won the Caldecott Medal in 1988, and “The Devil’s Arithmetic” (1988), about a Jewish girl who travels back in time to the Holocaust.

item.title: He Made ‘Heathcliff’ Absurd
item.description: Peter Gallagher’s spin on a comic strip staple has found a passionate audience away from the funny pages.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh
item.media:credit: Sydney Krantz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Peter Gallagher in his basement office, which he calls “the Dungeon.”

item.title: In Venezuela, a 91-Year-Old Weaver Sticks to Traditions
item.description: Though electric machines are now standard, the Venezuelan weaver Margarita Mora has clung to a mix of ancestral Indigenous and Spanish practices to create surprisingly modern work.
item.dc:creator: Silvia Benedetti and Andrea Hernández
item.media:credit: Andrea Hernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Listen to This Sondheim Masterpiece and It Will Bring Order to Your Day
item.description: Stephen Sondheim’s masterpiece, “Sunday in the Park With George,” contains a song capable of making order out of mayhem. Listen to hear how he did it.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Sunday in the Park with George (Play)

item.title: Dito van Reigersberg, Avant-Garde and Drag Virtuoso, Dies at 53
item.description: A co-founder of Pig Iron Theater Company, known for its surreal productions, he also gave energetic performances as his alter ego, Martha Graham Cracker.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: David Plowden, Who Photographed a Disappearing America, Dies at 93
item.description: With his haunting images of steam locomotives, steel mills and Midwestern farms, the celebrated lensman revealed the poetry in the artifacts of manual labor.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: Brazilians Revive a World Cup Ritual to Cheer on Their National Team
item.description: After years of dampened enthusiasm about the World Cup, Brazilians are excited about their national team again — and they’re showing it by painting the streets.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova and Dado Galdieri
item.media:credit: Dado Galdieri for The New York Times

item.title: At the Canadian Screen Awards, the Industry Steps Outside Hollywood’s Shadow
item.description: No longer content with being just “Hollywood North,” the Canadian screen industry is asserting itself and telling its own stories.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Cole Burston
item.media:credit: Cole Burston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The actor Robbie Graham-Kunz speaking with fans outside the 14th Canadian Screen Awards in Toronto.

item.title: Spielberg Gets Paranoid With ‘Disclosure Day’
item.description: Disclosure Day shows that after 50 years Spielberg’s fixations are the same, but his faith in the system has deteriorated.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Edward Vega and Alex Gallitano

item.title: Captain Sandy Keeps It Moving on Bravo’s ‘Below Deck Mediterranean’
item.description: Since 2017, the captain has led several megayachts and crews on the popular reality-TV franchise.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.media:credit: Fred Jagueneau/Bravo
item.media:description: Capt. Sandy Yawn, a maritime industry veteran of nearly four decades, has built a following as the star of Bravo’s “Below Deck Mediterranean.”

item.title: Gene Shalit, Film Critic Bristling With Hair and Puns, Dies at 100
item.description: One of the nation’s most recognizable characters, he delivered his wacky commentary for more than 40 years on the “Today” show.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:credit: Dave Pickoff/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gene Shalit in 1980. With his handlebar mustache, bushy hair, black horn-rimmed glasses and extravagant bow ties, he was one of the nation’s most recognizable characters.

item.title: Charles Dennis, a Founder of the Avant-Garde Space P.S. 122, Dies at 77
item.description: A performance artist, dancer, choreographer, videographer, filmmaker and curator, he was a central figure in the downtown Manhattan experimental arts scene.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: James Blood Ulmer, Guitarist Who Smashed Through Genres, Dies at 86
item.description: A protégé of the saxophonist Ornette Coleman, he borrowed from and greatly influenced styles like funk, punk, jazz and the blues.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Andrew Lepley/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: James Blood Ulmer in 1990. The New York Times called him “the most original electric guitarist to emerge since the late Jimi Hendrix.”

item.title: Review: Taking an Expressionist Class With the Royal Ballet
item.description: Paul Lightfoot and Sol León brought their blend of stretched balletic line and idiosyncratic gesture to London with a new work inspired by daily ballet class exercises.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas

item.title: Blake Lively Awarded Legal Fees in Ruling After Justin Baldoni Settlement
item.description: The size of the award remains to be determined. It only involves the legal fees associated with part of her court battle with Mr. Baldoni, with whom she made a film.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Eduardo Munoz/Reuters
item.media:description: A judge on Friday awarded Blake Lively some legal fees in her court battle with Justin Baldoni. The amount was not set in the ruling.

item.title: The Philharmonic and the Met Engage in a Battle of the Bands
item.description: Bruckner’s Eighth Symphony was performed in the same week by two of Lincoln Center’s orchestras. Was it coincidence or bad communication?
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Evan Zimmerman/Metropolitan Opera, Chris Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Yannick Nézet-Séguin, left, and Semyon Bychkov both conducted Bruckner’s Eighth Symphony in New York this past week.

item.title: Kennedy Center Removes Trump’s Name From Facade
item.description: The arts institution followed a judge’s order to take President Trump’s name off its facade. It had been granted a 12-hour extension to complete the work.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Pete Kiehart for The New York Times
item.media:description: On Friday night, workers constructed scaffolding near President Trump’s name on the facade of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts.

item.title: Framing David Hockney’s Greatest Art
item.description: Whether in Los Angeles, in his native England or traveling the world, the artist always reinvented the world he saw, with psychological insight.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter

item.title: Five Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include a wartime rescue, teenage assassins and Emma Thompson’s rescue skills.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels
item.media:credit: Vertigo Releasing
item.media:description: A still from “Beast,” featuring Daniel MacPherson.

item.title: Taylor Swift Enters the Songwriters Hall of Fame With Tears, Jokes and a Warning
item.description: After an introduction from Steven Spielberg, Swift gave a lengthy speech encouraging artists to “trust their human intuition” in an industry driven by data.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: In a speech, Taylor Swift called songwriting “pretty much the only thing I ever just naturally did.”

item.title: Opera Company Sues to Collect $17 Million From the Kennedy Center
item.description: The Washington National Opera, which left the center amid the Trump administration’s takeover, says its efforts to retrieve its endowment and other assets have been blocked.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Kennedy Center and the Washington National Opera are no longer affiliated, but they remained entangled in a dispute over what assets the opera might still be owed.

item.title: Joe Negri, Handyman and Music Maestro on ‘Mister Rogers,’ Dies at 99
item.description: An accomplished jazz guitarist, he appeared on more than 300 episodes of the public television show, playing alongside musicians like Wynton Marsalis and Yo-Yo Ma.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: JR Conjures a Mountainous Illusion Out of the Pont Neuf in Paris
item.description: Four decades after the conceptual artist Christo draped the Pont Neuf with fabric, JR, a French street artist, offers his own trick of the eye.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Elliott Verdier
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times
item.media:description: The bridge has been wrapped with fabric that was then inflated to create a craggy appearance.

item.title: What I Learned From David Hockney
item.description: The curator Norman Rosenthal knew the artist for over 60 years and still discovered something new when they collaborated on a final blockbuster show.
item.dc:creator: Norman Rosenthal
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Journalists looking at David Hockney’s work before the opening of the exhibition “David Hockney 25” at Fondation Louis Vuitton in Paris in April 2025.

item.title: With iPhones and Faxes, David Hockney Embraced Tech
item.description: Polaroids and photocopiers also gave the artist possibilities for creating in forms vastly different from his paintings.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: David Hockney
item.media:description: “Mulholland Drive, June 1986,” a homemade print from a photocopier.

item.title: David Hockney, Who Restored the Human Form to Art, Dies at 88
item.description: His colorful figurative paintings were both conservative and iconoclastic, defying the dominant abstract schools of the mid-20th century.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter

item.title: The Pianist Igor Levit Bets Big on Recordings With His Own Label
item.description: Despite woes in the recording industry, Levit announced the founding of No Silence, which will release three albums this fall.
item.dc:creator: Anastasia Tsioulcas
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: The pianist Igor Levit, who is partnering with his longtime label, Sony, on the new imprint No Silence.

item.title: 7 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: How ‘The Furious’ Somersaults Through a Wild Action Scene
item.description: The new movie is packed with showstopping action, but one standout sequence has our hero rolling over a pyramid of henchmen. Here’s what it took.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: The Books Times Readers Are Most Excited About This Summer
item.description: Lush historical fiction, gripping thrillers, true crime, laugh-out-loud essays and more: Here are the books you’ve saved most to your reading lists.
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Disclosure Day’ | Anatomy of a Scene
item.description: The director Steven Spielberg breaks down an action sequence from his new film “Disclosure Day” for Anatomy of a Scene.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy, Sutton Raphael, Gabriel Blanco and Edward Vega

item.title: ‘The Gas Station Attendant’ Review: A Daughter’s Questions
item.description: Karla Murthy retells her Indian dad’s life story to better understand her own in this deeply personal documentary.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Late Night Prepares for Trump’s U.F.C. Birthday Fight at the White House
item.description: Jimmy Fallon called the president “the only 80-year-old yelling, ‘Get on my lawn!’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Review: ‘Romeo & Juliet’ Find Too Little Love in the Park
item.description: For the Public Theater’s Shakespeare in the Park, the director Saheem Ali presents a strangely low-energy version of the tragedy.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Ra’Mya Latiah Aikens as Juliet, Francis Jue as Friar Lawrence and Daniel Bravo Hernández as Romeo in the play “Romeo and Juliet” at the Delacorte Theater in New York.

item.title: Kennedy Center Appeals Order to Remove Trump’s Name
item.description: One day before a deadline to take the president’s name off its facade, the arts institution appealed a federal judge’s ruling that also temporarily blocked it from closing.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Kevin Lamarque/Reuters
item.media:description: Judge Christopher R. Cooper ruled that only Congress had the power to alter the name of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, which was dedicated to the president in a 1964 law.

item.title: William Coupon, Who Wanted to ‘Photograph Everyone in the World,’ Dies at 73
item.description: Self-taught, he became a go-to portraitist for politicians, rock stars and other celebrities. He also documented Indigenous people and inmates on death row.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: ‘North Star’ Explores Frederick Douglass’s Ties to Ireland
item.description: Hunted by slavers, the abolitionist escaped briefly to Britain and Ireland. A new show at the Irish Arts Center combines his speeches with performances by local students.
item.dc:creator: Tim Teeman
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: “North Star,” directed by the British D.J. Kwame Daniels, recently opened at the Irish Arts Center in Manhattan.

item.title: Boston Symphony in Crisis Over Dismissal of Andris Nelsons
item.description: Chad Smith, the orchestra’s president, admitted missteps in terminating Andris Nelsons’s contract but stood by the decision and won’t step down.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney and Robin Pogrebin

item.title: ‘Doctor Who’ Is in Limbo. What Does That Mean for Fans?
item.description: The BBC said there would be no Christmas special this year and is looking for a production company to take on the sci-fi classic. Followers have already dubbed this uncertain period “the Wilderness Years 2.0.”
item.dc:creator: Isobel Lewis
item.media:credit: Francesca Jones for The New York Times
item.media:description: Russell T Davies, the showrunner who rebooted “Doctor Who” in 2005.

item.title: Duane Michals, Artist of Wit and Courage
item.description: Our conversations over the years were, at times, philosophical, metaphysical, honest about the daily circumstances of our lives, and dishy, a photography critic says, as he looks back.
item.dc:creator: Philip Gefter

item.title: For Painting’s Great Skeptic, Gerhard Richter, History Is a Blur
item.description: Our critic Jason Farago on how the artist conceived a new way forward for landscape painting.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago

item.title: Orlan’s Face Is Her Canvas, and Her Calling Card
item.description: Orlan transformed herself through plastic surgery in the 1990s for an art project. Now, she is lecturing at the Louvre about changing perceptions of beauty.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Orlan, with horn-like cheek implants in her forehead, has also created what she calls “bio-art” performances.

item.title: ‘Stop! That! Train!’ Review: Gleefully Going Off the Rails
item.description: RuPaul stars as the American president, who must contend with an unfolding transit crisis and drag queens galore as a dangerous storm approaches.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi
